proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Jesus Simulator as a 'narrative visual novel experience' that retells the Gospel through dialogue, key story moments, multiple viewpoints, and interactive mini-games. The text emphasizes authored historical narrative, spiritual reflection, and witnessing events rather than combat, systems-driven simulation, or open-ended RPG play. Single-player mode is directly supported by store metadata. Although one metadata label mentions 'First-Person Adventure' and generic RPG/simulation tags appear, the detailed gameplay copy is much more specific and strongly supports visual_novel as the core identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Historically grounded, educational interactive narrative built around branching dialogue, memories/comics, and light minigame-like scenes; very close to the anchor's story-first structure.
Choice-driven historical narrative focused on witnessing major events with cinematic scenes, exploration, and light interactive set pieces; somewhat more adventure-oriented than the anchor.
Strong historical narrative and dialogue focus with meaningful choices, but its investigative adventure structure is more systemic and less visual-novel-like than the anchor.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante
staged_neighbors.requested_title: The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very text-forward and choice-driven with strong morality/faith themes, but it is a stats-heavy narrative RPG in a fantasy setting rather than a historical visual novel retelling.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: The Lion's Song: The Full Season
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Historical, episodic, text-led narrative with shifting character perspectives and light interactive storytelling.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently identify a level-based motorcycle racing game centered on clearing fast-paced tracks, weaving through traffic, avoiding obstacles, chasing record times, and completing skill objectives. The core loop is explicitly vehicular racing with vehicle control, speed management, track hazards, unlockable levels, and score/time-performance goals rather than simulation realism. The copy also describes 45 levels, collectible symbols, and chapter unlocks, supporting an arcade-style campaign structure with replayable stage mastery. '3D runner' phrasing adds obstacle-dodging flavor, but the dominant identity remains arcade racing.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest gameplay fit: motorcycle-focused, level-structured, precision obstacle navigation, stunt/physics inputs, and repeated runs to improve performance. It differs in side-on physics format and broader multiplayer emphasis, but the mastery loop is very aligned.

Very strong on high-speed survival racing, obstacle-dense tracks, jumping, boosting, and finishing stages quickly rather than lap racing. Car/flying mechanics and a more abstract traversal model make it less direct than Trials.

Good adjacent comp for reflex-heavy neon driving, obstacle avoidance, and runner-like stage design. However, it is lighter and more abstract, with less motorcycle-specific handling and less race-structured progression depth.
Arcade motorcycle course racing with stunt control, hazards, and medal-based replay across compact levels.
Precision bike obstacle stages with restart-heavy time mastery and skill-based track progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official text is sufficient and consistent. Steam explicitly calls RUBATO an 'experimental, 2D physics-based take on the collect-a-thon genre' and says players move through 'open metroidvania-like areas' across '5 compact, densely packed areas' while uncovering 'secrets and shortcuts in every corner of the world.' That strongly supports a side-scrolling exploration-platformer structure with semi-open interconnected spaces and discovery gating, which fits metroidvania better than the 3d_collectathon archetype because the game is explicitly 2D. The description also mentions interacting with enemies and objects ('use your tongue to lick enemies and objects') and collecting Planet Bits to fix the solar system, supporting moderate action layered onto exploration. Narrative framing is authored and character-focused, but gameplay identity is primarily side-scrolling exploratory platforming.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: 2D exploration-platforming built around physics interactions, collectible progression, and interconnected metroidvania-like areas.
Offbeat 2D action-adventure with humorous writing, side-scrolling exploration, questing, and compact world discovery.
Side-scrolling platform exploration through interconnected zones with ability-gated progression and collectible-focused advancement.
Modern collectathon centered on dense exploration, secret hunting, and compact worlds, even if its perspective is 3D rather than side-scrolling.

current_live_neighbors.title: Blast Brigade vs. the Evil Legion of Dr. Cread

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently describe a single-player action-horror campaign built around piloting a weaponized hot air balloon across a chain of islands, scavenging resources, repairing and upgrading the vehicle, managing limited med kits, and fighting failed experiments with mounted weapons plus close-range axe/shotgun combat. The game emphasizes survival pressure, hostile horror theming, freely flying between islands, and objective/gig-based progression toward powering towers and reaching a sealed facility. This strongly supports action_horror as the best fit, with survival-adventure and open-world action-adventure traits as secondary influences. Perspective and exact camera are not stated, so those fields are left empty.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on vehicle-centric survival: flying craft as home/base, scavenging ruined locations, managing hull integrity, upgrading the ship, and exploring a hostile world. Less horror-forward and more crafting/science sandbox, but still the clearest fit.

Open-world survival shooter with a weaponized mobile base at the heart of traversal, resource gathering, repairs, upgrades, and combat. Strong structural match, though the drillship/base-building focus is heavier and the traversal fantasy differs from aerial roaming.

Train-as-home progression, resource gathering, crafting, combat, and movement between floating destinations map well to the anchor's mobile-vehicle survival loop. More co-op survival sandbox and less horror/action-hybrid than the anchor.

Shares moving-base survival progression, island visits, scavenging, and constant maintenance pressure. However, it is much more systemic survival crafting and less combat-driven, less horror-oriented, and less focused on a weaponized vehicle.
Vehicle-centric survival where your ride is the upgrade path, resource sink, and lifeline through hostile anomaly-filled runs.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a single-player horror-themed strategy/adventure game centered on commanding specters, solving puzzles, and resource management across distinct locations. However, the available text does not cleanly support any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The gameplay reads closest to a god-game / tactical puzzle / strategy hybrid, which is outside the permitted archetype list. While there are strong signals for horror setting, tactical optimization, and unit control, key identity facets needed to force-fit an allowed archetype are missing or contradictory: no reliable perspective, no clear RTS base-building/economy loop, no turn-based tactics framing, no shooter/action framing, and combat itself appears indirect rather than a dominant conventional combat loop. Given the apparent taxonomy gap and lack of a strong allowable match, rejection is the safest outcome.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Strongest fit here: horror-flavored indirect-control strategy with trap setup, monster abilities, victim manipulation, and planning-heavy outcomes. More construction/management driven than Ghost Master, but audience overlap is clear.

Shares indirect defense, trap networks, minion specialization, and villain-side management, but its core loop is a broader lair-building sim with economy and base expansion, farther from Ghost Master's supernatural scenario-puzzle structure.
Stage-driven indirect control game where you trigger abilities, hazards, and chain reactions to solve tactical scenarios rather than fight directly.
Centers on manipulating a haunted house, setting up scares, and using environmental interactions to terrorize intruders in contained spaces.
A dark tactical game about commanding a roster of specialized undead units with resource management and ability synergies, albeit in turn-based combat.

title: RUSHING BEAT X: Return Of Brawl Brothers

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and directly identify the game as a beat 'em up/brawler: 'ultimate Beat 'em up,' '2D Beat-'Em-Up,' with 9 stages, enemy-filled areas, 2-player co-op story mode, multiple playable fighters, auto-combos, counterattacks, follow-up strikes, throws, jumps, dashes, cancel system, rage gauge, and a finishing move. This strongly supports a classic side-scrolling stage-based beat_em_up identity with dominant melee combat and combo-driven crowd encounters.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Modern belt-scrolling brawler focused on accessible co-op play but with deeper combo routes, juggles, cancels, weapons, and distinct fighters.
A more technical 2D beat 'em up emphasizing cancels, branching combo strings, throws, and mastery-heavy crowd control within classic stage-based brawling.
Classic arcade-style co-op beat 'em up with urban levels, weapon pickups, throws, and a straightforward but skill-based hand-to-hand combat loop.
Co-op side-scrolling brawler with character-specific move sets, chaining attacks into longer combos, and a more expanded progression structure layered onto the core beat-'em-up loop.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify The Coin Game as an open-world collection of ticket-redemption arcade activities with realistic physics, carnival attractions, laser tag, mini-golf, karting, prize redemption, scavenging for money, and light survival-style needs management. The evidence is strong about the game's premise, but it does not cleanly align with any available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. Top computed candidates like sports_sim, pinball, and party_game are partial fits at best and fail to capture the product's core identity as an open-world arcade/minigame sandbox. Because the available archetype set lacks a good match and forcing one would misclassify the gameplay identity, rejection is the safest outcome.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Arcade-management and playable arcade-machine focus, with lots of minigame cabinets tied to money, score chasing, and a nostalgic leisure-space sim loop.
Closest mainstream comp for the ticket-redemption and midway-minigame side of the experience, with score-driven carnival attractions and prize-minded play.
Roaming a resort-like island packed with varied casual activities and transport options mirrors the freeform leisure sandbox structure.

current_live_neighbors.title: Call of the Wild: The Angler

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam descriptions consistently identify this as a basketball game centered on 5v5 matches, local multiplayer PvP, and a 1-player story mode. The core loop is explicitly match-based sports competition: '5 on 5 Pro Basketball,' 'side-scrolling, sweaty arcade action,' 'simplified 3-button gameplay,' 'dynamic play-calling,' large rosters with individualized attributes, and local multiplayer. While it has retro arcade presentation and a light story/unlock structure, the dominant identity is still a sports game built around winning basketball matches rather than a general party format. Secondary party-game adjacency is supported by local multiplayer and casual pickup-and-play framing, but sports_sim is the clearer fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: accessible arcade basketball, quick competitive matches, exaggerated play, and broad roster appeal. It differs in 2v2 structure and a more spectacle-driven style, but it matches the anchor's pick-up-and-play sports identity well.
Modern arcade basketball counterpart with simplified inputs, NBA rosters, quick exhibition structure, and local multiplayer.

title: Dynasty Warriors 3 Complete Edition Remastered

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored publisher/store text consistently describes Dynasty Warriors-style '1 vs. 1,000 action' on battlefields with over 40 officers, True Musou Attacks, evolved action combat, officer development, challenge modes, and two-player simultaneous play. Metacritic explicitly labels it a '3D Beat-'Em-Up,' which aligns with the core loop of third-person melee crowd-clearing combat across staged battles. The game also includes officer progression and tactical battlefield framing, supporting a light secondary resemblance to character action, but the dominant identity is musou/beat-'em-up style melee crowd control.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Dynasty Warriors 8: Xtreme Legends Complete Edition
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Dynasty Warriors 8: Xtreme Legends Complete Edition
staged_neighbors.why_similar: A definitive Dynasty Warriors package with the same crowd-clearing musou loop, officer roster focus, story campaigns, upgrades, and extra Xtreme Legends content.
Omega Force’s sister musou series with the same large-map hack-and-slash battles, character campaigns, base capture, and officer-driven progression.
Another Omega Force musou centered on combo-heavy 1-vs-thousands combat, huge character roster management, and mission-based battles with co-op support.

missing_must_include_titles: Dynasty Warriors 8: Xtreme Legends Complete Edition

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Crimson Desert as a third-person open-world action-adventure/RPG set in a medieval fantasy world, with free exploration across wilderness, cities, ruins, and the Abyss. Core play includes questing, battles, challenging enemies and bosses, multiple weapons and skills, elemental enhancements, dodges/counters/grapples, and traversal via climbing, gliding, and horseback. Progression includes character growth, skill development, equipment, and rebuilding/upgrading the Greymane camp, supporting an action-RPG identity over pure action-adventure. The strongest fit is open_world_fantasy_action_rpg, with secondary overlap into open_world_action_adventure and some soulslike signals from punishing boss-focused combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: third-person single-player fantasy action RPG with open-world exploration, varied weapon/class playstyles, large monster fights, and traversal emphasis; party/pawn structure is the main difference.
Strong match on dangerous open-world exploration, buildcraft, mounted traversal, and demanding boss-centric combat, though it is more purely soulslike and less quest/camp-driven than the anchor.

Good fantasy action RPG comp with open-world exploration, challenging large-enemy encounters, vocation-based progression, and real-time combat, but it is older, more party-defined, and less structurally aligned with Crimson Desert's broader modern open-world feature set.
Shares third-person open-world fantasy questing, horseback travel, and action combat, but its identity is more narrative/choice-driven RPG than boss-forward action system showcase.
Single-player mythic fantasy action RPG centered on challenging boss fights, melee-focused build progression, and high-commitment third-person combat.

current_live_neighbors.title: The Legend of Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom
The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Scritchy Scratchy as a scratch-card incremental/idle-style progression game centered on buying scratch-offs, revealing symbols, unlocking auto-scratching, taking risks on higher-variance cards, and prestiging for permanent upgrades. That establishes a clear gameplay identity, but it does not map cleanly to any available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not supported as pinball, racer, sports, card battler, management tycoon, or other listed archetypes, and forcing a fit would misclassify the title. Rejecting is appropriate because the game appears to sit outside the current archetype set rather than lacking evidence.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on idle/incremental structure: manual actions become automation, numbers scale rapidly, and prestige/permanent upgrades drive long-term progression.
Strong fit through luck-driven payout loops, run-building synergies, and gambling-style variance, though it is more roguelite run-based than idle/prestige incremental.
Good neighboring comp for passive income, automation managers, offline growth, and reset-based progression, but its business-scaling loop is less tactile and less luck-variance driven.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present DigDigDrill as a single-player underground mining and resource-collection game focused on digging for minerals and treasure, returning to the surface when inventory is full, and forging/customizing drills through a puzzle-like smithing/build-synergy system. The text explicitly says mining occurs 'without enemies or time limits,' which weakens any combat-driven archetype despite the 'hack-and-slash style equipment system' phrasing. The dominant loop appears to be relaxed resource gathering, build crafting, and progression through deeper floors, but the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a clear non-combat mining / digging / crafting progression archetype that this cleanly fits. Accepting would require forcing the game into a combat, survival, sandbox, or management identity not strongly supported by the evidence.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best match for dig-deeper resource runs and upgrade-driven underground progression. It differs by being a combat-heavy platform metroidvania, but mining and equipment-led advancement still overlap strongly.
Shares underground mining, ore collection, crafting, and long-term power growth. Fit is loosened by its sandbox-survival/base-building focus, multiplayer profile, and much broader activity set.
2D descent-based mining loop with return-to-town upgrades and equipment-driven drilling progression.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: A Game About Digging A Hole
staged_neighbors.requested_title: A Game About Digging A Hole
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Low-pressure dig-sell-upgrade structure focused on relaxing excavation and gear improvement.
Upgrade-driven drilling deeper for minerals with hauling and equipment progression at the core.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies Defending Camelot as a tower defense game explicitly compared to Plants vs. Zombies, with 160 campaign levels, unit unlocking, coin collection, power-ups, and waves of monsters prevented from entering Camelot. That is strong evidence for tower defense gameplay, but tower defense is not represented by a suitable archetype in the provided Similar Games Taxonomy V2 list. Available archetypes like rts or side_scrolling_action_strategy would misclassify the core gameplay identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The defining comp: lane-based tower defense, staged waves, varied defender roster, progression through many levels, and explicit audience overlap.
A very close tower defense match with campaign structure, unit/tower specialization, spell support, enemy-type counterplay, and long-form level progression.
Clearly in the same tower defense family, but its freer map interaction, top-down control, and co-op-focused presentation make it a somewhat looser fit than PvZ or Kingdom Rush.
Polished campaign tower defense with hero abilities, enemy counters, upgrades, and map-by-map progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently supports a non-combat, cozy single-player handcrafted puzzle game focused on coloring stained glass, rule-based logic puzzles, secret optional puzzles, and a level editor. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes do not include a general logic/puzzle-game bucket. The system-generated near miss for hidden_object_puzzle is not well supported by the actual description, which emphasizes rule-based handcrafted puzzles rather than object-finding scenes. Because the strongest evidence indicates a puzzle identity that does not cleanly map to an allowed archetype, the title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate classification.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent fit on handcrafted solo puzzle progression, layered rule discovery, exploration, and optional secrets. Less cozy/artisanal and more open-world first-person, but the mastery curve is very aligned.
Strong match for relaxed, curated, non-combat puzzle play with elegant mechanics, discovery, and off-path optional content. Its push-block/island structure is different, but audience overlap is high.

Very good pure-logic comparison: hand-designed, rule-complete deduction with escalating challenge and no guessing. However, it lacks the anchor's cozy presentation, exploratory structure, and artistic restoration feel.
Stained-glass themed puzzle assembly directly matches the anchor’s art-restoration fantasy and relaxed solo play.
Cozy art-construction puzzler focused on filling patterns under simple constraints rather than reflex play or combat.

title: The Ratline

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a single-player detective mystery built around examining documents, photographs, and artifacts, following leads, and making deductions with no hand-holding. Source labels include Point-and-Click, and the computed evidence strongly supports cursor-driven interaction, puzzle gating, environmental puzzle solving, authored linear structure, mystery-solving goals, and no combat. That aligns best with hidden_object_puzzle in this taxonomy, despite the game emphasizing deduction/investigation more than literal hidden-object scenes.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: The Case of the Golden Idol
staged_neighbors.requested_title: The Case of the Golden Idol
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Deduction-first mystery design built around inspecting scenes and documents, then filling in conclusions through logical inference.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: The Rise of the Golden Idol
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Modern investigative puzzle sequel with the same clue-linking, evidence parsing, and no-hand-holding deduction loop.
Investigative mystery focused on digging through records, cross-referencing evidence, and making independent logical leaps.
A landmark deduction game where observation and reasoning drive progress, with minimal combat and strong puzzle gating.
Evidence-driven mystery solving through searching, interpreting fragments, and piecing together the truth yourself.

missing_must_include_titles: The Case of the Golden Idol

current_live_neighbors.title: Harold Rabbit - Finder of Lost Things

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store descriptions consistently present a business-management simulator loop: rent and sell movies, organize inventory, hire staff, manage returns and fees, decorate and expand the shop, and respond to seasonal customer demand. This strongly supports management_tycoon over city_builder/colony_sim because the focus is operating and optimizing a commercial venue rather than urban planning or autonomous colony survival. Evidence also clearly supports single-player, non-combat, sandbox-style progression with base expansion and sim-oriented challenges.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: First-person retail management with stocking shelves, cashier tasks, layout optimization, and gradual store expansion.
Task-driven first-person business sim where you directly serve customers, restock, hire workers, renovate, and expand services.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store and aggregator copy consistently describes a train-driving simulation built around taking the controls of Thomas and other engines from an interactive first-person cab view. The game emphasizes carrying passengers, hauling freight, shunting, running timetable services, and a free-roam mode, with stories and exploration across Sodor. It is explicitly described as being powered by Train Sim World with realism/simulation technology but accessible controls, strongly supporting transport_sim over racing or adventure archetypes. Single-player only, non-combat, vehicle-control-centric, and simulation-driven structure are all directly supported by the provided text.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Same core first-person train-driving sim foundation, with scenario-based runs, timetable services, and accessible cab operation.
Another close match in Dovetail’s route-based train-sim format focused on driving services and curated scenarios from the cab.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently and concretely describe a first-person survival horror campaign set in Sker Hotel where the player must stay hidden, manage breathing and noise, evade enemies that hunt by sound, solve puzzle mechanisms, explore for clues and notes, and use a limited defensive device only to stun enemies and escape. The copy repeatedly names the game 'survival horror' and emphasizes stealth, close-quarters tension, exploration, and multiple endings shaped by clues, which strongly supports survival_horror as the core gameplay identity. Stealth-action and psychological horror elements are present, but the dominant loop is survival-focused horror exploration and evasion rather than action combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct flat-screen counterpart with the same core loop: first-person stealth survival horror, sound-sensitive enemies, limited defensive device, clue-driven exploration, and multiple endings.
Story-driven VR horror with first-person exploration, vulnerability, and environmental interaction, but it is more psychological narrative horror than stealth-survival systems horror.

Close-quarters VR horror with investigation and object interaction fits the immersive vulnerability side, though its episodic exorcism/investigation structure is looser than Maid of Sker VR's stealth-evasion survival loop.
VR first-person survival horror in a claustrophobic hotel with puzzle exploration, scarce resources, and constant vulnerability.
VR stealth-horror focused on hiding, environmental interaction, puzzle solving, and surviving threats with little direct combat.

removed_candidates: Resident Evil 7: Biohazard - End of Zoe

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and corpus text consistently describe a top-down Survivor-style action roguelite built around 1-4 player co-op runs, fighting hordes of enemies, stacking upgrades each run, combining weapons and trinkets, and culminating in boss battles. The core loop is much closer to a co-op action roguelite than beat-em-up or RPG alternatives. Strong signals include explicit 'top-down roguelite action,' 'Survivor-style combat,' high replayability, solo and up-to-4-player co-op, unlockable upgrades that grow stronger every run, and diverse enemies plus epic boss battles. Fantasy kingdom/warrior framing supports high_fantasy and heroic tone.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Defining survivor-like loop of surviving swarms, stacking upgrades each run, and outlasting escalating enemy waves in compact arena maps.
Closer action-heavy survivor formula with active dodging, large enemy hordes, boss encounters, and deep build combinations across multiple characters.
Top-down co-op roguelike with hero-specific kits, melee/ranged combat, map objectives, and major boss showdowns anchoring each run.
Arena survival structure focused on hordes, loot/build growth, character differentiation, and periodic boss fights within replayable runs.
Fast top-down 1-4 player co-op roguelite emphasizing distinct heroes, real-time combat, synergistic upgrades, and boss-centric progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions are consistent and strong: this is a single-player survivors-like/top-down shoot-'em-up action roguelike built around repeated runs, endless enemy waves, bullet-hell dodging, procedurally generated weapons, stacking upgrades, and build-driven survival. However, the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a clean fit for a single-player survivors-like/top-down action roguelite shooter. Available shooter and roguelite archetypes are materially mismatched (for example, hero shooter, military FPS, tactical shooter, extraction shooter, loot shooter, or co-op action roguelite). Because the gameplay identity is clear but does not map reliably to an allowed archetype, this title should be rejected rather than force-fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Definitive wave-survival roguelite with escalating enemy swarms, rapid upgrade drafting, and run-based power snowballing.
Closest match on survivors-like structure, bullet-heavy arena chaos, build stacking, boss waves, and endless-style scaling.
Another compact arena survival roguelite centered on surviving waves through fast buildcraft, item synergies, and constant stat tuning.
Dark fantasy survivors-like focused on kiting huge hordes, ability synergies, and long-form progression within repeated survival runs.
More manual twin-stick shooting and dodging, but very similar in bullet-hell pressure, short runs, and weapon-centric upgrade builds.
